channel.title: computer-science on mahmood
channel.description: Recent content in computer-science on mahmood

item.title: data structures
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) for most data structures i havent implemented deletion yet.. also its good to note that most of the code isnt optimized to hell, but it doesnt really matter since its only for education purposes and you should probably be using the data structures provided by the programming language’s standard library 1 properties of for every 2 numbers \(m,n\) and every number \(x\) \[ \ceil*{ rac{\ceil*{ rac{x}{m}}}{n}} = \ceil*{ rac{x}{m \cdot n}} \qquad  loor*{ rac{ loor*{ rac{x}{m}}}{n}} =  loor*{ rac{x}{m \cdot n}} \] \[ \ceil*{ rac{\ceil*{ rac{x}{2^i}}}{2}} = \ceil*{ rac{x}{2^{i+1}}} \qquad  loor*{ rac{ loor*{ rac{x}{2^i}}}{2}} =  loor*{ rac{x}{2^{i+1}}} \] the following is true for every normal number \(n\) and any number \(x\)  egin{gather}  loor*{x}+n =  loor*{x+n} \qquad \ceil*{x}+n=\ceil*{x+n}\ n- loor*{x} = \ceil*{n-x} \qquad n-\ceil*{x} =  loor*{n-x}\  loor*{x} \geq \ceil*{x}-1 \qquad \ceil*{x} \leq  loor*{x}+1\  loor*{x} > x-1 \qquad \ceil*{x} < x+1\  loor*{x} \leq x \qquad \ceil*{x} \geq x\  loor*{x} = n \iff n \leq x < n+1 \qquad \ceil*{x} = n \iff n-1 < x \leq n \end{gather}

item.title: algorithm correctness
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(\def	extsc#1{\dosc#1\csod} \def\dosc#1#2\csod{{m #1{\small #2}}}\) an algorithm is totally correct if it receives valid input, terminates, and always returns the correct output 1 proving algorithm correctness by induction the only way to prove the correctness of an algorithm over all possible inputs is by reasoning formally or mathematically about it. here we’ll be using mathematical induction we need to prove the correctness of this algorithm, and we do so using induction for each \(i=1,2,\dots,k\), when the algorithm arrives to the end of the loop for the i‘th time, the following loop invariant holds true: \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j] \] with the loop invariant in mind, we use induction on the i+1‘th iteration, we would be adding the value A[i+1] to the variable sum, so we know on the i+1‘th iteration the following is true: \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j] + A[i+1] = \sum_{j=1}^{i+1} A[j] \] which is what we needed to prove 2 correctness of recursive algorithms consider the following function proof that the algorithm terminates: assume that \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}\) runs on an array A, on each recursive call, the size of the input array A is halved, its obvious that at the end of every sequence of calls the size of the input array would arrive to 1 which is the edge case that would cause the function to terminate proof of correctness: we prove this by induction on the number of elements in the array for \(|A|=1\) which is the edge case, the algorithm returns \(A[1]\), the only element in \(A\), its obvious that the output is correct

item.title: Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) these are my notes on reading the book which covers the science of artificial intelligence extensively 1 solving problems by searching a solution is an action sequence, so search algorithms work by considering various possible action sequences. the possible action sequences starting at the initial state form a search tree with the initial state at the root; the branches are actions and the nodes correspond to states in the state space of the problem an additional data structure could be used to keep track of nodes that have been already visited in the tree to prevent redundant repetitions, demonstrated in the GRAPH-SEARCH algorithm which expands on the TREE-SEARCH algorithm (the new lines are highlighted) 2 machine learning 2.

item.title: networking
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) this document is still being constructed, it should not be used for reference yet. i have yet to add citations from the syllabus of the course from syllabus.docx 1 storage units basic units table: unit description bit 1 or 0 byte 8 bits kilobyte 1024 bytes megabyte 1024 kilobytes gigabyte 1024 megabytes terabyte 1024 gigabytes petabyte 1024 terabytes 2 unit prefix table prefix exponent yocto \(10^{-24}\) zepto \(10^{-21}\) atto \(10^{-18}\) femto \(10^{-15}\) pico \(10^{-12}\) nano \(10^{-9}\) micro \(10^{-6}\) milli \(10^{-3}\) kilo \(10^3\) mega \(10^6\) giga \(10^9\) tera \(10^{12}\) peta \(10^{15}\) exa \(10^{18}\) zetta \(10^{21}\) yotta \(10^{24}\) 3 internet at home there are 4 computers and a router (therefore, 5 “stations”) connected to the same channel.
